steps.title: Identify the appositive type
steps.content: 'Thymol and methane' specifies which compounds are being discussed (the sentence has stated only 'gaseous compounds' generally). It's restrictive — essential to identify the subject — so no commas should set it off.

steps.title: Apply the rule
steps.content:
The structure: "The gaseous ______ have molar masses..." The blank should contain the noun phrase "compounds thymol and methane" — these are the two specific compounds named (an essential appositive; only two exist, so the names are restrictive, not parenthetical). The rule: restrictive appositives take NO comma; non-restrictive (parenthetical) appositives need commas on both sides.
  • Choice A — "compounds, thymol and methane": opening comma without closing comma — incorrectly treats the names as non-restrictive while leaving the appositive unclosed. ✗
  • Choice B — "compounds, thymol and methane,": commas on both sides treat the names as parenthetical, but they are essential (they identify which compounds). ✗
  • Choice C — "compounds thymol and methane": no commas — treats the names as a restrictive appositive that identifies the specific compounds. ✓
  • Choice D — "compounds thymol and methane,": trailing comma with no opening comma — mismatched and creates an awkward break before the verb "have." ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
